coredata.dc:title: A copper response fluorescent probe for selective detection of GSH based on Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zuo, He
Hou, Xingyu
Sun, Na
Sun, Xue
Lv, Yuguang
Ding, Lixin
Li, Ze
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) is a biological sulfide that participates in various physiological processes. The development of highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensing analysis methods is of extraordinary significance. As a natural precursor, green tea and fragrans were used to synthesize double-emitting N-doped carbon dots (Nd-CDs). Aimed to detect GSH, an “dual switch” fluorescent probe was constructed with copper ions (Cu2+). The nanoprobe exhibits outstanding specificity and a linear range spanning from 0.1 to 100 μM in relation to Cu2+, accompanied by a limit of detection (LOD) of 14.5 nM. Due to the dynamic quenching process, the emission at 505 nm showed a special “turn-off” fluorescence sensing, while that at 436 nm was significantly elevated. Furthermore, GSH exhibits a strong attraction to the surface of Cu2+, leading to the formation of aggregates due to the chelating effect, resulting in fluorescence recovery of Nd-CDs with the sensing assay achieved a broad linear range of 0.1–80 μM and LOD 23.9 nM. Ultimately, the Nd-CDs@Cu2+ fluorescent probe was effectively utilized for the determination of GSH in tomato, blood serum and cell lysates, indicating the potential applicability of the nanoprobe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
GSH

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Admeasurement of GSH in different kinds of samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Jiamusi University Youth Innovative Talents Training and Support Program project (JMSUQP2023012)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization images of fluorescent probes (A) TEM containingsize distribution (B) Lattice spacing (C) Particle size distribution (D) XRD (E) FT-IR (F-I) XPS.

FLorescence response analysis images (A) Add fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of Cu2+ (0.1–100 μM) (B) Linear relationship between Cu2+ concentration and logarithm of fluorescence intensity(C) Add fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of GSH (0.1–80 μM) (D) Linear relationship between GSH concentration and logarithm of fluorescence intensity.
(A) Mechanism of GSH detection (B) UV–vis spectra and fluorescent spectra of Nd-CDs (C) PL decay curves.
The selectivity capabilitiesNd-CDs@Cu2+ for different compounds.
The Nd-CDs applied for GSH determination (A) preparation and detection process (B) “dual switch” mode diagrammatic sketch.
Comparison of analytical performances for the determination of GSH.

head.ce:title: A copper response fluorescent probe for selective detection of GSH based on Nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, Jiamusi 154007, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Jiamusi University, Jiamusi 154007, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A desirable ratiometric fluorescent probe (Nd-CDs) was applied to GSH detection, drug detection in real samples with tomato, blood serum and cell lysate leaves samples.

Glutathione (GSH) is a biological sulfide that participates in various physiological processes. The development of highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensing analysis methods is of extraordinary significance. As a natural precursor, green tea and fragrans were used to synthesize double-emitting N-doped carbon dots (Nd-CDs). Aimed to detect GSH, an “dual switch” fluorescent probe was constructed with copper ions (Cu2+). The nanoprobe exhibits outstanding specificity and a linear range spanning from 0.1 to 100 μM in relation to Cu2+, accompanied by a limit of detection (LOD) of 14.5 nM. Due to the dynamic quenching process, the emission at 505 nm showed a special “turn-off” fluorescence sensing, while that at 436 nm was significantly elevated. Furthermore, GSH exhibits a strong attraction to the surface of Cu2+, leading to the formation of aggregates due to the chelating effect, resulting in fluorescence recovery of Nd-CDs with the sensing assay achieved a broad linear range of 0.1–80 μM and LOD 23.9 nM. Ultimately, the Nd-CDs@Cu2+ fluorescent probe was effectively utilized for the determination of GSH in tomato, blood serum and cell lysates, indicating the potential applicability of the nanoprobe.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (γ-glutamylcysteinyl-glycine, GSH) is an antioxidant non-protein biothiol that is incorporated into functional meals and medicine [1,2]. Many signaling pathways linked in different biochemical and physiological processes depend on its redox characteristics to be regulated [3]. GSH is ubiquitous in cells, where it performs a variety of biological functions including deintoxication, metabolism, gene regulation, intracellular signal transduction and maintenance of intracellular redox activity [4]. However, increased levels of GSH in tumor cells can potentially aggravate pathophysiology, rendering them resistant to radiation treatment and chemotherapy in cancers of the bone marrow, breast, colon, larynx, and lung [5]. Thus, identifying upregulated molecules in cancer cells, known as cancer biomarkers, may offer a reliable approach for tumor diagnosis. One notable cancer biomarker exhibits significantly elevated levels in malignant tumors when compared to normal cells [6]. Consequently, monitoring GSH levels in biological systems is critical for the early diagnosis of cancer.

The significance of GSH has driven many techniques for its detection to arise. Advanced instrumentations have greatly improved the sensitivity of GSH detection, including mass spectrometry (MS), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary electrophoresis, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), and electrochemical methods [7–9]. Traditional instrumentation does, however, have several disadvantages including complicated sample processing, expensive equipment expenses, and dependence on qualified experts. Because of the target-induced fluorescence variations, fluorescence imaging using exquisite probes can thus give cellular, molecular, and anatomical information. Owing to their special optical characteristics, fluorescence probes which includes metal–organic frameworks, fluorescent dyes, metal-based nanoparticles, and semiconductor quantum dots have so far been extensively used for the detection of GSH. Still, challenges such as photobleaching and biological and environmental toxins remain difficult problems. In biosensing applications, Carbon dots (CDs) [10] outperform fluorescent dyes and heavy metal nanomaterials. As a prominent category of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, they exhibit numerous outstanding properties, including excellent photoluminescence (PL), high resistance to photobleaching, low cytotoxicity, favorable biocompatibility, and a long half-life within cells.

Green synthesis and economical consumption, on the other hand, have consistently been the focal point of chemical synthesis and its applications, guiding to the use of renewable for researchers [11], readily available, and CDs with unique functions synthesizing by natural biomass which express high fluorescence, good water solubility, and low toxicity for fluorescence sensing [12]. Generally speaking, natural product-based CDs have low yield and weak fluorescence. Commonly used surface passivation and foreign atom doping help to enhance the fluorescence characteristics of CDs [13,14]. While doping with metal atoms is expensive and harmful, surface passivation is difficult and time-consuming. Low cost and toxicity of nonmetallic element doping provide a cheap and efficient substitute. Particularly nitrogen doping is beneficial since nitrogen's atomic size is similar to carbon and its five valence electrons are accessible for bonding, therefore boosting the natural features of CDs and increasing fluorescence [15]. Green tea and fragrans [16,17] are rich in organic compounds such as amino acids, polyphenols, and other nitrogen-containing molecules. These components naturally provide carbon and nitrogen, enabling the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Nd-CDs). It was in line with the principles of environmentally friendly and sustainable chemistry. Therefore, it is essential to use natural precursors for the Photoluminescence (PL) Nd-CDs in order to improve the value in the field of biomedicine.

In this work, biomass-derived Nd-CDs were synthesized using osmanthus and green tea as nitrogen and carbon source. Simultaneously a copper-responsive “dual switch” fluorescence sensor based on Nd-CDs (Nd-CDs@Cu2+) was also constructed (Scheme 1 ). Subsequently, the dynamic quenching mechanism of the manufactured fluorescent probes applied for selective GSH determination. Developed method's practical relevance was evaluated in tomato, serum, and cell lysate samples. Over existing techniques for GSH screening, this developed probe offers major benefits in terms of cost, environmental impact, and simplicity of synthesis.

Copper sulfate(CuSO4), ferrous chloride (FeCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium bromide (KBr), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), calcium chloride (CaCl2), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), and zinc chloride (ZnCl2) were purchased from Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). GSH, Histidine (His), Aspartic acid (Asn), Valine (Val),Folic acid (Fa), Sialic acid (acid), Lysine (Lys), Glutamic acid (Glu), Arginine (Arg), and D-Serine (Ser) were purchased from Rhawn (New York, NY, USA). Phosphate buffered solution (PBS) was purchased from Lonza (Shanghai, China). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and quinine sulphate (QS, 98 %) were obtained from Sigma (New York, NY, USA).

An electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd., Japan) was used to characterise the morphology of Nd-CDs nanocomposites. An X-ray diffractometer (XRD-6000, Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan) was used to measure the structures. FT-IR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to measure the functional groups and chemical composition of Nd-CDs. FT-IR spectra were recorded with a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer using KBr as a template (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). The XPS analyses were performed using a Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250Xi (Waltham, MA, USA.). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured useing with a Hitachi U-3900 spectrophotometer.

A 1:2 mixture of green tea (0.2 g) and fragrans (0.4 g) was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, then stirring for 1 h at 25 °C afterwards. The solution was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, sealed in a stainless-steel hydrothermal reactor, and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was collected from the reactor. To isolate purified Nd-CDs, the solution was first subjected to centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 20 min) followed by filtration through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove residual aggregates. Solution was concentrated by rotary evaporator and vacuum dried at 80 °C. Powdered samples were obtained for further studies.

The quantum yield (QY) of Nd-CDs was calculated using the relative measurement method. Quinine sulphate (QS, QY = 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4) was used as reference fluorophore for Nd-CDs. The quantum yields were calculated by the following equation (1):
(1)
Φ S
=
Φ R
×
I S
I R
×
A R
A S
×
η S 2
η R 2

In the quantum yield (Φ) calculation, Φ denotes the fluorescence quantum efficiency, I corresponds to the integrated emission intensity, η represents the refractive index of the aqueous medium (η = 1.33), and A signifies the absorbance value. Subscripts S and R indicate the sample and reference fluorophore, respectively.

Due to the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the synthesized Nd-CDs were utilized for the detection of Cu2+ and GSH. A solution of 1 mg/mL Nd-CDs were prepared in deionized water. The fluorescence emission spectra of Cu2+ ions, at concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 100 μmol/L, were recorded over the wavelength range of 410 to 640 nm with an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra of GSH were examined by adding GSH, at concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 80 μmol/L, to the Nd-CDs@Cu2+ system. The selectivity of the Nd-CDs@Cu2+ complex towards GSH was assessed in the presence of various metal ions and amino acids. All measurements were conducted under controlled conditions.

To measure the concentration of GSH in tomatoes, the edible portion was crushed and combined with deionized water. This mixture was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min and subsequently filtered using a 0.22 μM membrane. The extract was stored at 4 °C before GSH levels were measured [15]. For HepG-2 cell lysate, 1 mL was placed in an ultrafiltration centrifuge tube (10 kDa) and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to separate large biomolecules [18]. To assess the GSH concentration in serum, 10 % trichloroacetic acid was employed to precipitate serum proteins. The mixture was then centrifuged to collect the supernatant, which was filtered through a 0.22 μM microporous membrane and subsequently diluted 50-fold with a Tris-HCl buffer solution (50 mM, pH = 8.0) [2].

By TEM, the morphology of the as-prepared Nd-CDs was examined. As shown in Fig. 1 A, they appear to be dispersed in a quasi-spherical shape.The insertion of a high-resolution TEM in Fig. 1B further confirms the quasi-sphericity with lattice fringes at a distance of 0.22 nm, which is attributed to the D-spacing of the graphene (100) plane [19]. In addition, the inset of Fig. 1A indicated that the average diameter of Nd-CDs was 2.25 nm with a narrow size distribution ranging from 1.25-5.75 nm (Fig. 1C), as determined by TEM analysis. The High-Angle Annular Dark-Field (HAADF) images in Fig. S1A clearly presented the atomic level structure of Nd-CDs. The uniformly distributed nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) atoms on the surface of the carbon dots exhibited moderately bright regions. Due to the higher atomic numbers, Cu2+ showed as significant bright spots in the HAADF images, indicated that it was anchored to the Nd-CDs- surface through ligand bonds. And the crystal structure of Nd-CDs was further verified by XRD pattern. Fig. 1D [11] had a wide (002) peak intermediate near (2θ = 22.3°), which indicated that Nd-CDs had a graphite-like structure.

The chemical composition and functional groups of Nd-CDs were initially characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 1E, the peaks observed at 3433–3010 cm−1 and 1448 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H and C-N, respectively, indicating the successful doping of nitrogen in the Nd-CDs [20]. Additionally, the tensile vibration peaks of C-H and H-C-H were identified at 2950 cm−1 and 2889 cm−1, respectively. The characteristic vibrational absorption at 1676 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C=O [21,22]. The stretching vibration attributed to C=C/C=N bonds exhibits a pronounced absorption band at 1589 cm−1, indicating the presence of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons within the Nd-CDs [20]. In addition, C-N tensile vibration was detected at 1446 cm−1 and C-O tensile vibration was observed at 1109 cm−1. Furthermore, the absorption bands located at 707 cm−1 correspond to C-C-C stretching vibrations. The broadening of the absorption bands observed in the range of 530–770 cm−1 could be attributed to the coordination of Cu2+ with functional groups (e.g., carboxyl groups, amino groups) on the surface of the Nd-CDs [23–25]. The FT-IR outcomes indicate that –COOH, –NH2, and –OH groups were triumphantly leaded into the surface of the Nd-CDs.

XPS spectra were made further efforts registered to assay the surface structure and elemental composition of Nd-CDs. The investigation XPS spectrum demonstrates the presence of elements such as carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O) in Nd-CDs (Fig. 1F). The elementary composition of the Nd-CDs are C 71.72 %, O 20.54 %, and N 7.74 %. In the highresolution XPS spectra (Fig. 1G-I), the C1s spectrum proves the existence of C-C/C=C (284.8 eV), C-N/C-O (286.2 eV) and −O-C=O (288.0 eV). The N1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into one binding energy peak at 398.6 eV, C-N, respectively. The O1s XPS spectrum shows the principal C-O (532.4 eV) peak in contrast with that of C=O (531.1 eV) [19,26]. And the zeta potential data (Fig. S1B) further supported the conclusion that the initial potential of Nd-CDs was −20.93 mV, which increased to −10.78 mV after the addition of Cu2+, and the introduction of GSH restored to −18.49 mV. It might be due to the electrostatic adsorption of Cu2+ on the Nd-CDs surface and the chelation of GSH and Cu2+ [27], it proved that the material could detect GSH.

The outcomes of TEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS and zeta potential data analysis suggested that Nd-CDs surface contains –COOH, –NH2 and –OH functional groups, Cu2+was anchored to the Nd-CDs surface by forming stable coordination bonds (such as Cu-O or Cu-N), which changes the original vibration mode, meanwhile, it has good dispersibility and graphite-like structure. Nd-CDs@Cu2+ which provided a structural basis for the competitive coordination mechanism induced by GSH. These results collectively elucidate the probe's selective mechanism, supporting its bioanalytical applications.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescent spectra, different excitation wavelengths of Nd-CDs were shown in Fig. S2A-C., Absorption peaks were prominently observed at 271 nm and 320 nm in the UV–vis spectrum (Fig. S2A). The absorption peak at 271 nm was attributed from the sp2 domain to the π − π* transition, which was considered to be the highest nuclear state [21], and the wide absorption at 320 nm can be considered to be the surface state, corresponding to the n-π* transition of the C=O or C=N functional group on the surface of CDs [28]. At 436 and 505 nm, this Nd-CDs exhibit intriguing dual-emission behavior (Fig. S2B). The probe's unique fluorescence was likely due to the conjugated system and the doping of N atoms. Doping N atoms changes the electron structure and energy level in CDs due to different electronegativity and lone electron pairs, resulting in stronger PL. In Fig. S2C., it could be seen that the fluorescence intensity increases and then tends to flatten out with the increase of the excitation wavelength, and the emission wavelength position is shifted with the increase of excitation wavelength, which shows the significant excitation-dependent emission. The emission peaks are redshifted by about 49 nm at the excitation wavelengths of 370–420 nm, a property that may be fundamentally related to the jumping of electrons inside the Nd-CDs [29,30].

In this paper, we study the chemical and optical stability of Nd-CDs as sensing probes in diverse system. First, we studied the photostability of the Nd-CDs in NaCl solutions. As shown in Fig. S2A, using a series of NaCl solutions at concentrations 0–1.0 M, the fluorescence intensity of Nd-CDs remained stable, declaring that the prepared probes showed no aggregation or structural changes in the range of NaCl concentrations. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of the nanomaterials only downscaled by 8.98 % after 6 weeks at room temperature (Fig. S2B), indicating satisfactory stability of Nd-CDs.

To obtain Nd-CDs with the best luminescence properties important arguments such as reaction time, synthesis temperature and green tea/osmanthus mass ratio were optimized. Controlling the synthesis temperature is the key to obtain the optimal fluorescence performance of CDs. At 436 nm, the fluorescence intensity decreased at the reaction temperature below 200 °C. This may be due to the lower temperature not causing surface defects of the reaction material [31], or the reaction was incomplete (Fig. S3A). The fluorescence strength of Nd-CDs was satisfactory at the reaction temperature of 200 °C, but gradually diminished with the temperature increased, probably due to the higher temperature disrupting the structure of Nd-CDs or reducing the functional groups on their surface. In general, the reaction time is too short to synthesize quantum dots with excellent performance, while the reaction time is too long to prolong the synthesis cycle and reduce the production efficiency. Therefore, we investigated the synthesis time of Nd-CDs, as shown, the product fluorescence intensity reached a maximum when the reaction time was scheduled to 8 h (Fig. S3B). Consequently, the reaction time was fixed at 8 h, and the optimal amount of green tea and fragrans in the subsequent reactions was 1:2 (Fig. S3C).And the optimal amount of water was 30 ml(Fig. S3D).

The results show (Fig. S4A and Fig. S5A) that the fluorescence intensity of Nd-CDs gradually decreased in the pH (4.0–7.0), which is related to the deprotonation of the amino group on the surface of Nd-CDs. At pH 7.0, the lowest fluorescence intensity yields the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency. buffer solution was chosen for detection. As Fig. S4B and Fig. S5B shown, the fluorescence intensity decreases rapidly within 5 min, indicating that the detection can be completed quickly. The relative fluorescence intensity of Nd-CDs is best under the ambient temperature of 25 °C. This sensor can detect Cu2+ (Fig. S4C) and GSH (Fig. S5C) at room temperature.

In this work, label-free Nd-CDs were served as fluorescent sensors to monitor the changes in Cu2+ levels in real samples. Initially, Cu2+ was detected under the excitation wavelength at 400 nm with a PBS of 10 mM (pH = 7). As shown in Fig. 2 A, after the introduction of 0.1–100 μM Cu2+, the fluorescence intensity of Nd-CDs keep increasing at 436 nm, while them keep decreasing at 505 nm, indicating that Nd-CDs could respond effectively to Cu2+ through the fluorescence changes at the two emission channels (F436 nm / F505 nm). The fluorescence quenching efficiency F436/F505, linear relationship with concentration of Cu2+ (C, μM) at 0.1–100 μM (Fig. 2B), linear regression equation F436/F505 = 0.02025CCu2+ + 0.46307 (R2 = 0.9961), where F436 and F505 are the fluorescence intensity of Cu2+, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) of Cu2+ was calculated about 14.5 nM using the 3δ IUPAC criteria (3 s/k, the assumption is that the standard deviation of the blank solution obtained over 10 repeated experiments is s, and k is the slope).

To investigate the effect of GSH on the fluorescence behavior of the Nd-CDs@Cu2+ system, the Cu2+ concentration was fixed at 100 μM. Varying concentrations of GSH (0.1–80 μM) were introduced into the Nd-CDs@Cu2+ system, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded (Fig. 2C). The fluorescence of Nd-CDs@Cu2+ was diminished with the introduction of GSH, after increasing GSH, the value of F436/F505 reduced gradually. The linear relationship with concentration of GSH (C, μM) at 0.1–80 μM (Fig. 2D), linear regression equation F436 /F505 = -0.01115CGSH + 2.08171 (R2 = 0.9835), where F436 and F505 are the fluorescence intensity of GSH, respectively. On the basis of Nd-CDs@Cu2+ system, the LOD for GSH detection was 23.9 nM. These outcome indicate that Nd-CDs@Cu2+ system can be used as a “off–on” sensor for GSH detection in aqueous systems.

The current assay is compared with other GSH assays that have been reported as shown in Table 1 . The experimental results showed that the material was not only able to achieve a lower limit of detection comparable to other standard methods, but also greatly expanded the range of fluorescent materials for the detection of GSH. Compared with the rest of the probe structures, the structure of Nd-CDs was characterised by one-step synthesis, assembly and modification in a single step without the need for stepwise grafting. Therefore, our strategy was to explore a reliable detection method with easy operation, high sensitivity, and wide linear detection extent.

The optical properties and fluorescence mechanism of Nd-CDs were further demonstrated by studying the UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectrum, quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime of Nd-CDs. The mechanism of GSH detection in Fig. 3 A. Based on the reference compound quinine sulfate, the QY of the as-prepared Nd-CDs was determined to be 22.83 %. Compared with the UV–vis spectra and fluorescent spectra of Nd-CDs, the excitation and emission spectra do not overlap well with the absorption spectrum (Fig. 3B). As a consequence, the proposed quenching can mechanism excludes the role of internal filtration effect (IFE) and Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [40]. Simultaneously, the Stern-Volmer diagram presents nonlinear properties in the range of 0--100 μM Cu2+ concentration at 436 nm, indicating that the quenching process of Cu2+ on Nd-CDs probable a non-kinetic mechanism [41], the results suggested might be derived from the efficacious electron transfer between Nd-CDs and Cu2+. The restored fluorescence of Nd-CDs@Cu2+ was probably put down to the higher portfolio effect between the Cu2+ and GSH, which the coordination of Cu2+ with the amino and carboxyl groups on glutathione, which effectively blocks the charge transfer of the Cu2+-Nd-CDs. This interaction subsequently results in the fluorescence recovery of Nd-CDs. Therefore the fluorescence detection mechanism of Nd-CDs may belong to a process of dynamic quenching.

As shown in Fig. 3C, fluorescent lifetime measurements further support the mechanism we proposed. As anticipated, the lifetimes were various in the sensor system. The average life span of Nd-CDs, Nd-CDs@Cu2+ and Nd-CDs@Cu2+/GSH were measured to be 1.19 ns, 1.33 ns and 1.64 ns, separately. It was showed that the lifetime increased with the multiplication of Cu2+ and GSH, which further illustrated the quenching was a dynamic quenching process.

The sulfhydryl groups in GSH capable of born the complexes with metal ions [42]. A desirable inspection model for GSH was cultivated using the Nd-CDs@Cu2+ system on the basis of this property. The fluorescence quenching effect of Nd-CDs@Cu2+ recovered with the decrease of GSH concentration. Fig. 4 evaluates the selectivity of the Nd-CDs@Cu2+ system for GSH detection, including the sensing effects of metal ions (i.e., Mg2+, Fe3+ , Zn2+, Mn2+, Ca2+ and K+), amino acids (i.e., Val, Asn, Arg, Lys, Glu, Ser, His), and organic (i.e.,Fa, Acid) acids on the Nd-CDs@Cu2+ fluorescence response. Under identical conditions, amino acids and other metal ions exhibit relatively low reaction activities towards Nd-CDs@Cu2+, further confirming the probe’s specific recognition of GSH. It primarily due to their lack of sulfhydryl (−SH) groups and markedly reduced coordination capacity with Cu2+ and devoid of the chemically active sites required for selective binding. This work we combined Cu2+and Nd-CDs to construct a ‘dual-switch’ ratiometric fluorescent probe (Nd-CDs@Cu2+)for detected for GSH. This interaction mechanism may result from the chelation of copper ions with GSH at specific binding sites, effectively reversing the Cu2+-induced fluorescence quenching of Nd-CDs. These research findings further validate the specific recognition capacity of this nanoscale probe for glutathione.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Admeasurement of GSH in different kinds of samples
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments were enforcement to demonstrate the latent application of the different system for gauging the GSH levels in three kinds of samples, which tomato blood serum, cell lysate samples were analyzed using spiked methods. The Nd-CDs@Cu2+ results showed recovery rates ranging from 93.10 % to 104.2 % and a low relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 3.0 % in Table 2 . The utility of the method for detecting GSH in complex substrates is demonstrated.

In summary, a copper-responsive “dual switch” based on Nitrogen-doped carbon (Nd-CDs@Cu2+) fluorescence sensor was successfully synthesized. The fluorescence sensing mechanism was dynamic quenching process of the Nd-CDs and Cu2+, along with the chelating effect between Cu2+ and GSH. Further, using tomato, blood serum and cell lysate samples, their recoveries were in the range of 93.10 %-104.2 % with RSDs ≤ 3.0 %. The prepared fluorescent nanoprobes have the characteristics of fast response, wide linear range, high selectivity and high sensitivity. Meanwhile, this Biomass fluorescent probe provides opportunities in ion/biomolecular tracking, biological imaging, and cancer diagnosis, and this method has promising application in biomedical and biotechnology fields.

He Zuo: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Xingyu Hou: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Na Sun: Methodology, Data curation. Xue Sun: Methodology, Data curation. Yuguang Lv: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Lixin Ding: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Ze Li: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Ze Li reports a relationship with the Jiamusi University Youth Innovative Talents Training and Support Program project that includes: funding grants. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper..

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Jiamusi University Youth Innovative Talents Training and Support Program project (JMSUQP2023012)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113642.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots as a “turn-off-on” fluorescence probe for the detection of Hg2+ and GSH and cell imaging
Anal. Methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yang, M. Zhang, Y. Zhang, N. Wang, W. Bian, M.M.F. Choi, Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots as a “turn-off-on” fluorescence probe for the detection of Hg2+ and GSH and cell imaging, Analytical Methods 11 (2019) 5803–5809. https://doi.org/10.1039/C9AY01723B.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of KCl-doped lignin carbon dots nanoenzymes for colorimetric sensing glutathione in human serum
Sens Actuators B Chem

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, Q. Sun, Y. Ma, X. Jiang, N. Niu, L. Chen, Synthesis of KCl-doped lignin carbon dots nanoenzymes for colorimetric sensing glutathione in human serum, Sens Actuators B Chem 364 (2022) 1-9. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2022.131881.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multifunctional carbon dots for glutathione detection and Golgi imaging
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Liu, H. Cai, Z. Xu, J. Li, X. Weng, C. Liao, J. He, L. Liu, Y. Wang, J. Qu, H. Li, J. Song, J. Guo, Multifunctional carbon dots for glutathione detection and Golgi imaging, Talanta 259 (2023) 1-7. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.124520.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of glutathione and glutathione disulfide using liquid chromatography: A review on recent applications
